minimal_test: Run a small replication using one tool-integrated RL model and one code-data-trained model on a proof benchmark, then generate minimally perturbed versions of the same problems and compare answer accuracy, proof score, and consistency.

scope_conditions.method: tool-use or code-data training plus perturbation-based evaluation
scope_conditions.task: AIME/MATH-style answers versus olympiad full-solution proofs

evidence_gap: Still lacking are paired studies that evaluate tool-use and code-trained models on both perturbation robustness and proof-writing quality.

hypothesis: The apparent inconsistency comes from one metric operationalizing end-answer exact correctness while another operationalizes loosely judged response relevance, so prompting can improve reasoning traces without always improving the final answer hit rate.
mechanism: Multi-step prompts may elicit more on-topic intermediate reasoning and better use of context, which raises percent-based relevance/success judgments, but the same outputs can still miss a precise target answer and therefore fail stricter accuracy scoring.
predictions: Relevance gains exceed accuracy gains on the same prompted outputs.
Many prompted outputs score relevant but not exactly correct.
minimal_test: Collect a shared eval set of multi-step reasoning items with gold final answers and preserve the same model outputs under baseline prompting and enhanced prompting. Re-score every output twice: once with exact/fuzzy answer accuracy against the gold answer, and once with a blinded relevance rubric or judge model matching the percent-style criterion used in the cited work. Compare per-example disagreement rates and whether prompt-induced deltas are larger under relevance than under accuracy.
evidence_gap: The cited claims do not provide paired annotations showing whether percent means relevance, success, or exact correctness on the same responses.

hypothesis: The divergence arises because one metric is macro-averaged over subdomains or reasoning categories while another is micro-averaged over all examples, so prompting helps some reasoning niches but hurts others and aggregate conclusions flip depending on weighting.
mechanism: Prompting methods like SRP or evidence-guided prompting may strongly benefit temporally compositional or context-heavy items yet harm static symbolic items; macro averaging across categories can highlight balanced category gains, whereas micro accuracy or overall percent can be dominated by the largest or easiest categories and look negative.
predictions: Macro category score favors prompting more than micro overall score.
Prompting wins on context-heavy subsets but loses on static-symbolic subsets.
minimal_test: Using one shared evaluation set partitioned into annotated subdomains or reasoning types, generate outputs from the same model with and without the prompt method. Cross-score the exact same outputs with two aggregations: micro accuracy/percent over all examples and macro-averaged score across categories. Then inspect whether the sign of the prompt effect changes under the two aggregation rules and which subsets drive the reversal.
evidence_gap: The claims report improvements and degradations but do not state whether the published percent/accuracy numbers are macro or micro aggregated on comparable subset distributions.

hypothesis: The conflicting outcomes reflect a surface-form metric gap: one metric gives credit for semantically correct free-form answers after normalization or judge scoring, while the other requires exact string or option match, making prompting look better when it changes wording but not when scoring is brittle to phrasing.
mechanism: Prompting often lengthens answers, adds explanations, or reformats conclusions; semantic scoring can recognize the intended answer despite paraphrase, whereas exact-match style accuracy can mark the same response wrong if the final token, option letter, or concise canonical form is missing or altered.
predictions: Semantic scoring rates prompted outputs higher than exact-match scoring.
Exact-match errors cluster on verbose but answer-equivalent responses.
minimal_test: Take a shared set of prompted and unprompted model outputs for free-form multi-step reasoning questions with canonical gold answers. Score every output twice: first with strict exact match or option-match extraction, second with semantic equivalence scoring via normalization plus blinded human or judge-model adjudication. Quantify how often prompting changes only answer form, and test whether prompt gains appear under semantic scoring but shrink or reverse under exact matching.
evidence_gap: The available claims do not expose whether percent-based evaluations used semantic adjudication while accuracy-based evaluations relied on exact-match extraction.

hypothesis: The disagreement is driven by dataset composition: GPT-4o looks strong on a mixed visual-reasoning benchmark average but weak on the narrower, expert-heavy UGPhysics subset.
mechanism: If one paper averages across easier or more heterogeneous visual reasoning datasets while the other evaluates a concentrated physics dataset with higher prerequisite knowledge and less visual cue redundancy, the relative headline for GPT-4o can flip from best-overall to weak-on-task.
predictions: Gap shrinks on matched physics-only subsets
Averaged rank changes when benchmark mix is reweighted
minimal_test: Construct a matched evaluation using GPT-4o on (i) the physics-related subset of the visual reasoning benchmark from arxiv:2501.06186v1#c03 and (ii) UGPhysics items filtered to comparable modality and difficulty; then recompute performance after equalizing subject mix and difficulty bins. If the contradiction is mostly due to composition, the positive-vs-negative framing should weaken under the matched subset.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation across visual reasoning and physics reasoning benchmarks

evidence_gap: The current claims do not report per-category breakdowns, difficulty calibration, or overlap in modality/knowledge requirements between the two benchmarks.

hypothesis: The contradiction is caused by prompt and answer-format differences that differentially affect GPT-4o, especially between multimodal visual reasoning setups and physics QA setups.
mechanism: GPT-4o performance can change sharply when prompts vary in chain-of-thought allowance, multiple-choice formatting, image placement, unit-handling instructions, or strict answer extraction. A permissive or well-aligned prompt may yield a top average score, while a terse or extraction-sensitive format may depress measured accuracy on UGPhysics.
predictions: Accuracy rises with benchmark-harmonized prompt templates
Strict parsing hurts more on physics items
minimal_test: Run GPT-4o on both benchmarks with a single harmonized prompt template, identical system message, identical answer format constraints, and the same parsing logic; then rerun with each paper's original prompt format. If the contradiction diminishes under the shared prompt/protocol, prompt format is a plausible moderator.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation with benchmark-specific prompting and answer extraction

evidence_gap: Neither claim provides the exact prompt templates, system instructions, or answer-normalization rules needed to isolate formatting effects.

hypothesis: The papers are using different evaluation protocols and headline aggregations, so one reports a relative 'best closed-source average' while the other highlights absolute accuracy on a harder benchmark, creating an apparent conflict without a stable common effect sign.
mechanism: A model can be best among closed-source competitors on an averaged benchmark yet still have low absolute accuracy on a different benchmark. Differences in aggregation (macro-average vs single-dataset accuracy), comparator set, and metric definition can flip interpretation from positive to negative even when underlying item-level competence is unchanged.
predictions: Relative rank and absolute accuracy diverge under common scoring
Closed-source-only ranking changes little after protocol alignment
minimal_test: Re-evaluate GPT-4o and the same comparator models under a unified protocol: same benchmark subset, same metric (e.g., exact accuracy), same averaging rule, and same comparator pool. Report both absolute accuracy and relative rank. If the contradiction disappears once the evaluation protocol is held fixed, protocol differences are a credible explanation.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation where claims mix relative ranking and absolute benchmark accuracy

evidence_gap: The claims lack a shared comparator pool, common metric definition, and a directly comparable benchmark-level score computed under one protocol.

hypothesis: The reported percent score is likely an aggregate benchmark average across heterogeneous subtasks, while accuracy measures exact item correctness on a single dataset, so GPT-4o can look strong on one metric and weak on the other without contradiction.
mechanism: Averaged percent can pool normalized sub-scores or category-wise rates across easier and harder components, whereas accuracy on UGPhysics counts only exact correct answers on that dataset; the aggregation level changes what is being rewarded.
predictions: Cross-scored UGPhysics outputs will show low exact accuracy but higher normalized percent if partial/category averaging is applied.
Cross-scored benchmark-wide outputs will compress variance under averaged percent more than under per-item accuracy.
minimal_test: Collect the same GPT-4o item-level outputs on a shared evaluation set spanning the visual reasoning benchmark items and UGPhysics-style items, preserving per-item predictions. Score each output set twice: (1) exact accuracy at the item level, and (2) the benchmark's published percent aggregation procedure, including any category averaging or normalization. Compare whether GPT-4o's ranking/score improves under the aggregate percent scorer relative to exact accuracy on the same predictions.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation across benchmark-average and single-dataset settings

evidence_gap: The claim text does not specify whether the 71.8% percent is a simple accuracy, macro-average, or normalized composite, so the exact aggregation rule must be recovered from the source benchmark.

hypothesis: The percent metric may tolerate partial credit or non-binary grading, whereas accuracy is binary exact-match, causing the same GPT-4o answers to receive substantially different scores.
mechanism: If evaluation assigns fractional credit for partially correct reasoning, option narrowing, or near-miss outputs, percent rises even when final exact answers are often wrong; accuracy ignores these near-correct responses.
predictions: Many GPT-4o wrong-by-accuracy answers will still earn nonzero percent credit under rubric scoring.
The gap between percent and accuracy will be largest on multi-step reasoning items with partially correct intermediate content.
minimal_test: Run GPT-4o once on a shared held-out set of evaluation questions that elicit free-form answers, storing raw outputs. Then cross-score the identical outputs with two metrics: (1) binary exact accuracy using the official final-answer key, and (2) a rubric-based percent scorer that awards partial credit, either from the benchmark instructions or by converting human/judge rubric points to percentages. Measure how often accuracy=0 but percent>0 for the same response.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation with free-form or multi-step responses

evidence_gap: We do not yet know whether the visual reasoning benchmark's percent score includes partial-credit grading rather than plain correctness.

hypothesis: The percent score may be based on judge- or parser-mediated answer acceptance that is less sensitive to exact surface form than accuracy, so GPT-4o's verbose outputs are credited under percent but marked wrong under strict accuracy extraction.
mechanism: Surface-form mismatch matters when accuracy requires an exact option string or exact parsed answer, while a percent-style scorer or benchmark judge may accept semantically correct but differently formatted responses; the definitional gap is answer equivalence versus exact extraction.
predictions: Re-scoring the same raw outputs with semantic equivalence rules will raise percent more than exact accuracy.
Formatting-constrained re-prompts will shrink the divergence between percent and accuracy on the paired outputs.
minimal_test: Generate one shared set of raw GPT-4o outputs on evaluation questions without forcing terse formatting. Score those outputs twice: (1) strict accuracy using the dataset's canonical parser/exact-match rule, and (2) percent using a semantic-equivalence judge or the benchmark's acceptance protocol. Then repeat on the same questions with a constrained answer-format prompt; if the gap narrows, surface-form sensitivity is implicated.

scope_conditions.task: evaluation where answers are free-form but can be reduced to canonical labels

evidence_gap: The available claims do not report the answer parsing or judge protocol, so it is unclear whether formatting sensitivity differs across the two evaluations.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because the two communities index the same text+image failure mode under different task labels—visual reasoning treats it as prompt-sensitive modality alignment, while crisis response treats it as retrieval-augmented factual QA—so relevant papers are missed in keyword and citation searches.
mechanism: Terminology drift plus venue separation causes authors to compare within prompting/reasoning or RAG/QA lineages rather than across multimodal alignment studies, even though both report parameter-efficient gains from better text-image coordination.
predictions: Cross-citation is lower than shared-entity overlap suggests
Alignment-related terms differ while ablations target similar components
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on 2024-2025 papers containing text+image multimodal setups, coding title/abstract terms for prompting, alignment, retrieval, and crisis/reasoning; then compare shared entities versus citation overlap between the two clusters.

scope_conditions.method: prompting and RAG papers with explicit image+text inputs
scope_conditions.task: visual reasoning and crisis multimodal QA

evidence_gap: We still lack a term-level audit showing whether authors discuss the same subproblems but use non-overlapping vocabulary and references.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination may be strongest if crisis response imports hint prompting from visual reasoning, because the large gains from structured hints suggest some crisis QA errors come from weak decomposition of visual-text evidence rather than missing knowledge alone.
mechanism: Hint prompts can externalize intermediate constraints about which image regions and text snippets matter, reducing reasoning omissions before or alongside retrieval in multimodal QA.
predictions: Hint prompts improve CrisisMMD accuracy without changing retriever
Benefits concentrate on image-grounded questions over text-dominant ones
minimal_test: Add the VisuLogic-style hint prompting protocol to the HM-RAG or GPT-4o CrisisMMD evaluation, keeping retrieval fixed and comparing zero-shot direct answers versus hinted answers by task subtype.

scope_conditions.method: hint prompting layered onto existing multimodal QA systems
scope_conditions.task: CrisisMMD tasks requiring image-text evidence integration

evidence_gap: No small replication has isolated whether crisis-response gains currently attributed to architecture would partially appear from prompt-structured reasoning alone.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination may also run the other way: visual reasoning could benefit from crisis-response-style hierarchical multimodal RAG, and the disconnect persists because visual reasoning benchmarks are framed as closed-book reasoning so retrieval tools are treated as out-of-scope despite similar alignment bottlenecks.
mechanism: Methodological priors favor intrinsic reasoning in visual benchmarks and external evidence grounding in crisis QA, delaying recognition that retrieved visual-text exemplars or agent decomposition might repair the modest gains seen from chain-of-thought and scaling alone.
predictions: Small multimodal RAG boosts VisuLogic more than plain chain-of-thought
Retrieved exemplars help 7B models more than larger closed models
minimal_test: Construct a lightweight VisuLogic benchmark variant with a small retrieved pool of analogous image-text examples or subproblem notes, then compare direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and hierarchical multimodal RAG on the same items.

scope_conditions.method: hierarchical multimodal RAG or exemplar retrieval
scope_conditions.task: visual reasoning problems where external analogous evidence can be attached without altering labels

evidence_gap: Missing evidence is a controlled import study showing whether retrieval/decomposition methods transfer to visual reasoning rather than only domain QA.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because physics papers frame text+image problems as reasoning/evaluation benchmarks while crisis-response papers frame them as multimodal RAG systems, causing terminology drift around the same modality-alignment mechanism.
mechanism: Different task labels and success criteria hide overlap: physics emphasizes benchmark difficulty, pass@k, and reasoning accuracy, whereas crisis response emphasizes retrieval-augmented QA and deployment efficiency, so citation search and reviewer matching miss adjacent work.
predictions: Physics papers rarely mention CrisisMMD or multimodal RAG despite using text + image inputs
Importing HM-RAG retrieval into a physics benchmark improves image-grounded answer accuracy over plain prompting
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison of keywords/citations between PHYBench or UGPhysics papers and CrisisMMD/HM-RAG papers, then replicate a small subset of physics benchmark questions with HM-RAG-style hierarchical multimodal retrieval added before generation.

scope_conditions.method: multimodal RAG versus direct multimodal prompting
scope_conditions.task: text + image scientific-reasoning and crisis multimodal QA

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence showing whether retrieval actually helps physics items that require visual grounding rather than external factual lookup.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because venue separation and methodological priors lead physics researchers to value test-time scaling and self-consistency, while crisis-response researchers prioritize retrieval pipelines; cross-pollination would be to apply pass@k sampling from physics to crisis QA and apply retrieval decomposition from crisis QA to physics.
mechanism: Communities optimize different bottlenecks: physics assumes the model already contains needed knowledge and studies inference-time search, whereas crisis response assumes missing or time-sensitive context and studies retrieval orchestration; each therefore under-tests the other's tooling.
predictions: Pass@32 on CrisisMMD yields gains larger than majority voting alone for ambiguous multimodal questions
Hierarchical retrieval helps UGPhysics most on visually grounded or context-heavy items, not symbolic-only items
minimal_test: On a shared multimodal model, evaluate CrisisMMD with pass@1, vote32, and pass@32; separately, add HM-RAG-style hierarchical retrieval/decomposition to a labeled subset of UGPhysics or PHYBench and compare against direct prompting.

scope_conditions.task: multimodal question answering versus physics reasoning evaluation

evidence_gap: No paired study currently isolates whether gains come from retrieval, decomposition, or sampling budget when the same backbone model is used across both tasks.

hypothesis: A generational lag may be sustaining the disconnect: newer physics benchmark papers compare frontier multimodal or reasoning models, while crisis-response work still centers applied multimodal alignment and parameter-efficient RAG, so citations have not yet caught up even though both report GPT-4o-centered text+image baselines.
mechanism: When one community updates benchmark/model lineups faster than the other, shared baselines look superficially comparable but experimental culture diverges; this delays recognition that both are probing robustness of text-image alignment under domain-specific constraints.
predictions: Recent physics papers cite frontier reasoning models but omit applied crisis multimodal benchmarks even when GPT-4o appears in both
A small replication using the same GPT-4o prompting and scoring protocol across one physics set and one CrisisMMD task reveals correlated failure cases in image-text mismatch
minimal_test: Compare reference lists and baseline tables in recent physics and crisis-response multimodal papers for overlap in models, datasets, and citation age; then run GPT-4o on a small matched sample from a physics benchmark and CrisisMMD with harmonized prompting and annotate shared error types.

scope_conditions.method: shared baseline replication with harmonized prompts
scope_conditions.task: multimodal evaluation where both text and image are required

evidence_gap: We lack direct error-taxonomy evidence showing whether the same modality-alignment failures, rather than domain knowledge gaps, drive mistakes in both communities.

hypothesis: Accuracy is primarily measuring exact end-task correctness, while AM/mAM/mLGM reward partial step coverage and chain balance, so the same multimodal outputs can look weak on accuracy yet strong on reasoning-oriented aggregate metrics.
mechanism: A response that gets some sub-steps right (e.g., temporal or logical links) but misses one required final decision receives zero or low accuracy, whereas AM-family metrics can still assign credit for partially correct multi-step structure or balanced performance across subdimensions.
predictions: Long-video answers with one broken sub-step will score higher on mAM than on exact accuracy.
Models with balanced temporal/spatial partial success will outrank final-answer leaders under AM score.
minimal_test: Take a shared set of video or audio multi-step reasoning items and collect raw free-form outputs from the same multimodal models once per item. For each output, derive a final-answer label for exact Accuracy scoring and separately annotate or parse sub-step correctness needed to compute AM score and mLGM/mAM on the very same predictions. Then compare rank correlations and identify outputs with low Accuracy but moderate/high AM-family scores.
evidence_gap: The cited claims do not yet show paired per-example scores under both exact-answer and sub-step aggregate metrics for identical outputs.

graph_bridge.from: accuracy, am score, mlgm and mam, mlgm; mam

hypothesis: BLEU-4 is measuring surface n-gram overlap, while SPICE and percent-style reasoning success are closer to semantic content selection, so outputs with generic or template-like wording can look good on one metric and not the other.
mechanism: A model can match reference phrasing or frequent Tier-2 lexical patterns and gain BLEU-4 without expressing the right comparative evidence, while semantically grounded metrics such as SPICE or direct success percentages depend more on whether the key entities, relations, and contrasts are actually conveyed.
predictions: Tier-2 outputs with more stopwords and boilerplate will retain BLEU-4 better than SPICE.
Semantically correct paraphrases will gain SPICE or task percent while losing BLEU-4.
minimal_test: On a shared comparative-reasoning evaluation set such as CLD/ACD, save the same generated explanations from each model. Cross-score every output with BLEU-4 and SPICE, and if available also mark binary task success percent from the same outputs. To generate a paired set if references are sparse, create two controlled variants of each original output: a paraphrase preserving meaning and a lexicalized template preserving common n-grams; then rescore both metrics on those paired outputs.
evidence_gap: Existing claims report leaderboard-style metric outcomes but do not isolate whether lexical overlap versus semantic proposition matching causes the reversals on the same answers.

hypothesis: Accuracy is measuring strict final-form exactness and is highly sensitive to language or answer canonicalization, whereas semantic-generation metrics are more tolerant to equivalent content, creating apparent multimodal inconsistency especially across multilingual reasoning settings.
mechanism: When the model gives the right reasoning in a non-canonical language, script, synonym set, or answer format, exact Accuracy may mark it wrong; metrics based on semantic graph overlap or graded generation can still reward the same output if its meaning aligns with the reference.
predictions: Multilingual answers judged wrong by exact matching will recover more under semantic scoring than English answers do.
Reformatting the same prediction into canonical answer strings will improve Accuracy more than semantic metrics.
minimal_test: Use a shared multilingual visual-reasoning set and collect one raw prediction per item from the same multimodal models in each language. Score the identical outputs twice: first with the original exact Accuracy pipeline, then with a semantic metric adapted from SPICE or human equivalence labels. Next canonicalize each prediction by normalizing scripts, numerals, and synonymous answer phrases without changing meaning, and rescore both metrics to see whether the accuracy gap shrinks disproportionately.
evidence_gap: The current claims show language-linked accuracy drops, but there is no paired evidence showing whether those same outputs would fare better under a meaning-tolerant metric.

hypothesis: The apparent contradiction is moderated by training-data composition: fine-tuning helps when examples are selectively hard and diverse, but hurts when the fine-tuning set is dominated by teacher-style, imitative reasoning traces.
mechanism: Hard-example selection can force policy improvement and error correction, while homogeneous expert traces encourage superficial format imitation and pseudo-reasoning; this can flip fine-tuning from beneficial to harmful on multi-step reasoning.
predictions: Hard-sample curricula outperform random SFT on transfer reasoning.
Imitation-heavy traces reduce downstream RL gains.
minimal_test: Construct two matched fine-tuning sets with the same model, token budget, and optimizer: (a) challenging/diverse samples selected by difficulty or response-diversity criteria, and (b) easy or teacher-imitative samples matched for size and domain. Then run the same subsequent evaluation or RL stage on the same multi-step-reasoning benchmarks; if the contradiction is due to data composition, performance differences between papers should shrink when both use the same curated set.

scope_conditions.method: fine-tuning, especially SFT or RFT with sample selection

evidence_gap: The papers do not provide a common audit of difficulty, diversity, and teacher-trace imitation rates in their fine-tuning corpora.

hypothesis: The sign of the fine-tuning effect is moderated by model scale and modality: smaller or weaker base models benefit from fine-tuning, whereas larger aligned vision-language models are more prone to rigidity and diminished post-SFT learning.
mechanism: Fine-tuning can supply missing reasoning structure to smaller models, but in stronger aligned LVLMs it may overconstrain latent search behavior, causing imitation lock-in and weaker improvement or worse RL adaptation.
predictions: 7B-class models show larger fine-tuning gains than 72B-class models.
Negative SFT-to-RL effects are strongest in aligned LVLMs.
minimal_test: Run the same fine-tuning recipe and evaluation protocol across a controlled scale ladder and modality-matched family (e.g., 7B and 72B text-only models, and 7B and 72B LVLMs) using the same reasoning data. Compare pre/post fine-tuning gains and post-SFT RL gains; if scale/modality is the moderator, the disagreement should lessen within the same scale-modality bucket.

scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning in text and visual settings

evidence_gap: There is no head-to-head study holding data and protocol fixed while varying only parameter scale and text-vs-vision modality.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by evaluation protocol differences: papers measuring single-stage benchmark accuracy after fine-tuning see gains, while papers measuring compatibility with subsequent RL or robustness of reasoning trajectories can observe degradation.
mechanism: Fine-tuning may improve answer formatting and immediate benchmark accuracy under one decoding/evaluation setup, yet simultaneously worsen exploration, calibration of chain structure, or RL compatibility under another protocol, reversing the reported effect.
predictions: Accuracy gains can coexist with weaker downstream RL improvement.
Sign changes under process-based vs answer-only evaluation.
minimal_test: Evaluate the same fine-tuned checkpoints under a unified protocol that includes both answer-only benchmark accuracy and a fixed downstream RL phase, with identical prompting and decoding. If the contradiction comes from protocol mismatch, studies should agree once both report the same pair of endpoints on the same checkpoints.

scope_conditions.method: fine-tuning pipelines compared with or without subsequent RL
scope_conditions.task: multi-step-reasoning benchmarks where both final-answer and process-sensitive outcomes are measurable

evidence_gap: Current claims mix reward, benchmark accuracy, and post-SFT RL outcomes without a shared evaluation suite or common decoding settings.

hypothesis: Selection-oriented metrics make tool-use look strong even when end-task completion is weak, because they score whether the model picked plausible tools rather than whether the full agentic objective was achieved.
mechanism: Metrics such as DTSR, tool precision, or partial tool-match reward local tool-choice correctness, while ACC, EMR, and overall success require the entire action sequence and final state to be correct; a model can therefore score high on selection but low on completion if later arguments, execution order, or stopping behavior fail.
predictions: High DTSR outputs will include many low-ACC cases with correct first tool but failed completion.
Re-scoring the same traces with EMR will penalize stop_early and argument mistakes more than TP/DTSR.
minimal_test: Collect one shared set of full tool-call traces and final answers from the same model on one agentic benchmark with step logs preserved. For each trace, compute both a selection metric (e.g., DTSR or TP/TF1 over chosen tools) and a completion metric (e.g., ACC or EMR) on the same examples. Then stratify disagreements by error subtype using the trace: correct tool chosen but wrong arguments, premature stop, or wrong final state.
evidence_gap: Need per-example paired scores linking tool-choice correctness to final task correctness, not just separate aggregate tables.

hypothesis: Exact-match completion metrics and token-overlap tool metrics measure different granularity: one requires the whole multi-step plan to be exactly right, while the other gives partial credit for overlap in tool sets or calls.
mechanism: EMR/overall success act like all-or-nothing sequence metrics, whereas TF1 and related overlap scores reward partial recovery of needed tools even with extra or missing calls. This definitional gap can reverse rankings, especially on multi-tool and multi-turn problems where partial plans are common.
predictions: Models with extra redundant calls will lose EMR more sharply than TF1 on the same items.
Rank correlation between TF1 and EMR will drop as tasks require more tools or turns.
minimal_test: On a shared eval split containing single-tool, multi-tool, and multi-turn items, save the exact same model outputs including ordered tool traces. Re-score each output twice: once with exact-match completion (EMR or success) and once with overlap-based scoring (TF1 over tool calls). Compare per-example metric gaps and model ranking changes across difficulty buckets defined by number of required calls/turns.
evidence_gap: Missing joint per-instance annotations showing when outputs are partially correct enough for TF1 but not exact enough for EMR/success.

hypothesis: Failure-profile metrics emphasize why a run failed, whereas success metrics collapse all failures together, so cautious and eager tool-use policies can appear contradictory depending on whether refusal/wrong-name/value-error rates are inspected separately or absorbed into one success score.
mechanism: Refusal rate, wrong-name error, stop_early rate, and value_error rate are decomposed error channels; overall success or accuracy ignores this composition and only records whether the run ended correctly. A cautious model may have lower dangerous wrong-tool errors but worse success due to refusals, while an eager model may improve success on easy cases despite worse semantic error rates.
predictions: Among the same outputs, refusal-heavy models will show lower wrong-name error but not necessarily higher success.
Conditioning on non-refusal cases will shrink gaps in success for cautious models while exposing higher value/semantic errors in eager models.
minimal_test: Run one model under fixed prompting on a shared benchmark and retain all outputs, including abstentions, tool names, arguments, and final answers. Cross-score every example with both a success metric (ACC/overall success/EMR) and decomposed failure metrics (refusal, wrong-name, stop_early, value_error). Then compare conditional success after excluding refused cases and inspect whether disagreement comes from different failure compositions rather than different underlying capability.
evidence_gap: Need paired example-level labels for abstention, tool-name correctness, argument validity, and final task outcome on the same traces.

hypothesis: The reported Percent metric is likely a relative-improvement or normalized score over a baseline, while Accuracy is absolute exact correctness, so the same RAG outputs can look strongly positive on Percent even when absolute answer correctness remains modest.
mechanism: A normalized percent score amplifies gains from a weak baseline and can reward partial progress across datasets or systems, whereas accuracy counts only whether the final answer is exactly correct; this definitional gap makes incremental retrieval benefits appear larger under Percent than under Accuracy.
predictions: Examples with low baseline performance show larger Percent gains than absolute Accuracy gains.
Ranking methods by Percent improvement differs from ranking them by final Accuracy on the same questions.
minimal_test: Take one shared factual-QA evaluation set with saved per-question outputs from at least two RAG variants and their stated baselines, preferably FaithEval or CRAG-style data. For each output, score final answer exact Accuracy against gold answers, then compute the paper-style Percent metric on the same outputs by reproducing the reported normalization or relative-gain formula against the same baseline outputs. Compare per-model rankings and plot Percent gain versus absolute Accuracy change on the paired predictions.
evidence_gap: The exact operational definition of Percent is not given consistently across claims, so the normalization formula must be recovered from each paper before deciding whether this mismatch fully explains the divergence.

hypothesis: Accuracy is measuring strict final-answer correctness, while Percent is measuring a softer task score that gives credit for partially correct, evidence-aware, or preference-aligned responses, so RAG gains show up more clearly under Percent than under exact Accuracy.
mechanism: Retrieval can improve grounding, source usage, and nuanced handling of conflicting evidence without always producing the exact canonical short answer string; a softer percent-based score can reward these intermediate improvements, whereas accuracy collapses them to zero.
predictions: Outputs judged better supported by retrieved evidence gain more under Percent than under Accuracy.
Disagreements between metrics concentrate on conflict-heavy questions such as RAMDocs-style items.
minimal_test: Assemble a shared set of question, retrieved-context, and model-answer triples from one RAG system across FaithEval, AmbigDocs, and RAMDocs. Score each identical answer twice: first with strict exact-match Accuracy against the gold answer, second with the original Percent-style evaluator used in the cited work or a reproduced rubric that yields partial credit. Then inspect items where Percent is high but Accuracy is zero, and categorize whether they contain correct evidence use, hedging, or partially correct content.
evidence_gap: We still need the precise scoring rubric behind Percent and item-level annotations showing whether it rewards partial correctness, evidence faithfulness, or other non-binary properties.

hypothesis: Accuracy is mainly sensitive to final answer identity, whereas Percent is aggregating comparative gains across datasets or prompts and is therefore more rank-oriented and less sensitive to absolute difficulty; this causes RAG to appear successful overall even on hard datasets where absolute Accuracy stays low.
mechanism: On active, still-debated factual-QA settings, especially with conflicting evidence, a metric based on percentage improvement over another system can remain positive whenever RAG is better than baseline, even if all systems struggle in absolute terms; strict accuracy instead exposes dataset hardness directly.
predictions: On RAMDocs-like hard subsets, Percent shows positive gains while absolute Accuracy remains low for all systems.
Within the same output pool, dataset difficulty explains more variance in Accuracy than in Percent improvement.
minimal_test: Use a shared evaluation set partitioned by dataset difficulty or conflict level, ideally FaithEval, AmbigDocs, and RAMDocs, with the same saved outputs from one RAG model and one non-RAG baseline. Cross-score each answer with exact Accuracy and with the reported Percent improvement relative to the baseline on the same instances. Then compare metric trends by subset: test whether harder subsets depress Accuracy much more strongly than they reduce Percent gains, and whether subset rankings differ under the two metrics.
evidence_gap: Item-level paired outputs for RAG and baseline on the same datasets are needed to verify whether Percent is primarily tracking relative advantage rather than absolute success.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because math-reasoning papers frame gains as training or prompting innovations, while physics papers disproportionately frame results as benchmark difficulty and evaluation, making shared mechanisms look like separate problems.
mechanism: Terminology drift around evaluation protocol and task labels obscures overlap: math work discusses robustness, self-consistency, GRPO, and tool-use under reasoning, whereas physics work discusses challenge, pass@k, and benchmark originality under evaluation.
predictions: Physics papers cite fewer math-reasoning robustness papers than expected given shared pass@k/self-consistency terms
Re-labeling PHYBench/UGPhysics with math-style robustness taxonomy increases keyword overlap and nearest-neighbor retrieval
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison on abstracts and method sections from the cited math-reasoning and physics sets, measuring overlap before and after normalizing terms such as pass@k/self-consistency/test-time-scaling/evaluation protocol; then manually inspect whether nearest cross-community matches improve.

scope_conditions.task: text and text+image reasoning benchmarks in math-reasoning and physics

evidence_gap: Missing is a direct bibliometric audit showing whether cross-citations stay low after controlling for shared methodological vocabulary.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination could be strongest if physics benchmarks import math-reasoning test-time scaling and RL-derived sampling tools, because both communities appear limited by answer-selection under multiple sampled traces rather than by raw knowledge alone.
mechanism: Math-reasoning methods like GRPO and two-stage test-time scaling may improve physics by generating more diverse candidate chains and better selecting among them; conversely, physics-style detailed scoring could reveal whether math gains come from true reasoning or lucky final answers.
predictions: Applying math-style two-stage long/short rollout scheduling to PHYBench beats plain vote32 at fixed sample budget
Replacing binary math scoring with physics-style detailed error categories reduces apparent gains from pass@k on MATH-500
minimal_test: Small replication study: evaluate one open model on PHYBench with baseline pass@32 majority voting versus a math-inspired two-stage rollout schedule or GRPO-trained sampler at equal inference budget; separately rescore a subset of MATH-500 using PHYBench-like detailed dimensions and compare method rankings.

scope_conditions.task: reasoning under sampled chains in text and text+image settings

evidence_gap: There is no head-to-head study holding sample budget fixed while comparing majority voting against math-style rollout scheduling on physics tasks, nor a rescoring study on math with detailed physics-style rubrics.

hypothesis: A methodological-priors split may sustain the disconnect: math-reasoning treats symbolic scaffolds and tool-use as central, while physics assumes modality alignment and physical perception are the bottlenecks, so each side underestimates the other's dominant failure mode.
mechanism: Math papers report gains from code data, distillation, and tool integration, while physics papers emphasize multimodal challenge and low absolute benchmark accuracy; this can create a prior that techniques successful in math will not transfer unless perception is solved first.
predictions: Tool-integrated reasoning yields larger gains on text-only UGPhysics subsets than on image-heavy physics subsets
Physics-style multimodal alignment training improves multimodal math benchmarks more than text-only math benchmarks when model size is fixed
minimal_test: Import math tool-use into a physics benchmark by running ToRL-style calculator/code execution on a text-only slice of UGPhysics and compare against no-tool baselines; in the reverse direction, import a physics-oriented modality-alignment module or multimodal curriculum into MathVista/GEO and compare to a text-only math-tuned baseline.

scope_conditions.task: text-only versus text+image physics and math reasoning

evidence_gap: What is missing is a controlled ablation separating symbolic-computation benefits from perception/alignment benefits across matched text-only and multimodal subsets.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because both communities study intermediate reasoning but package it under different modality-specific terms, while a useful bridge would import math robustness perturbation protocols into robotics CoT-style VLA evaluation.
mechanism: Terminology drift around the same latent construct: math papers discuss chain-of-thought, logical consistency, and perturbation robustness in text or text+image reasoning, whereas robotics papers discuss visual goals, reachability, and action sequencing; this hides a shared concern about whether intermediate representations remain stable under small input changes.
predictions: Robotics papers will rarely cite math perturbation-robustness work despite similar failure descriptions.
Applying hard input perturbations to VLA prompts or frames will reduce CoT-VLA success disproportionately on tasks requiring multi-step plans.
minimal_test: Compare vocabularies and citations between math-reasoning papers on perturbation robustness and robotics VLA papers on intermediate reasoning; then run a small replication by adapting math-style original-vs-modified evaluation protocol to a CoT-VLA benchmark with text edits and mild visual perturbations while measuring plan consistency and task success.

scope_conditions.task: text or text+image math reasoning and manipulation tasks with multi-step plans

evidence_gap: Direct side-by-side measurements of perturbation sensitivity for explicit reasoning traces versus end-task success are missing across the two communities.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because venue separation and benchmark customs keep tooling siloed, while cross-pollination would look like importing robotics-style modality alignment representations into multimodal math reasoning and importing math RL reasoning curricula into VLA planning.
mechanism: Different evaluation ecosystems reward different artifacts: robotics emphasizes grounded spatial alignment and action-relevant state abstractions, whereas math emphasizes benchmark accuracy and reasoning-token optimization; because recent activity is low, the gap may reflect independent maturation rather than active disagreement.
predictions: Adding reachability-style structured visual state to multimodal math benchmarks with diagrams will improve geometry performance more than algebra performance.
Using GRPO-style reasoning optimization for intermediate visual-goal selection in VLAs will improve simulated planning success before real-world success.
minimal_test: Run a focused literature comparison of benchmark sections and venue citations for multimodal math versus embodied VLM/VLA papers; then perform two small imports: add an S-P-Map-like structured alignment channel to a geometry/mathvista-style benchmark, and train a compact VLA planner with GRPO over intermediate visual-goal choices on a simulation benchmark.

scope_conditions.method: multimodal alignment modules or PPO-or-GRPO training
scope_conditions.task: diagram-heavy math reasoning and simulated manipulation

evidence_gap: There is little direct evidence showing whether structured spatial grounding transfers to symbolic visual reasoning, or whether RL reasoning objectives transfer cleanly to action-conditioned planning.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because methodological priors differ on what counts as a useful tool or demonstration, and cross-pollination would involve testing math tool-integration ideas in robotics and robotics short-horizon rollout ideas in math agents.
mechanism: Math-reasoning work often treats external tools and distilled traces as assets for exactness, while some math evaluation results show demonstrations can hurt and robustness is uneven; robotics instead prefers short action horizons and grounded execution, so each community may discount the other's helper signals as domain-specific rather than instances of the same assistance-vs-overfitting tradeoff.
predictions: Tool-integrated RL will help robotics manipulation most on tasks with latent arithmetic or symbolic subgoals, not pure reactive control.
Constraining math agents to short-horizon intermediate commitments akin to action chunks will reduce demonstration-induced errors on perturbed problems.
minimal_test: Replicate a small subset of manipulation tasks with a calculator/planner or verifier tool exposed during policy rollout, and separately evaluate a math agent on perturbed MATH-style items using short-horizon staged reasoning outputs instead of long free-form demonstrations; compare error types and success rates to no-tool and standard-CoT baselines.

scope_conditions.method: tool-use, staged reasoning, or short-horizon decomposition
scope_conditions.task: symbolically rich manipulation tasks and perturbation-sensitive math benchmarks

evidence_gap: Missing are controlled studies that isolate when helper signals become scaffolds versus distractions across text-only and embodied settings.

hypothesis: Accuracy-like metrics may reward correct intermediate operations, while average success rate/percent on agentic-reasoning may reward only end-to-end task completion, making the same outputs look strong on one metric and weak on another.
mechanism: An agent can execute many local steps correctly yet fail the overall plan; cross-scoring the same trajectories would therefore produce higher operational-accuracy scores than end-to-end success scores, especially on multi-step or composite items.
predictions: On shared trajectories, step accuracy exceeds task success most on composite items.
Models with similar accuracy can separate sharply on end-to-end success.
minimal_test: Collect a shared eval set of agentic-reasoning items with saved full action traces from one or more agent systems. For each output, score twice: (1) operational/step accuracy against gold intermediate actions or verifier checks, and (2) end-to-end task success / percent solved. Then compute per-item and per-model gaps, stratifying by basic vs composite tasks.
evidence_gap: A paired benchmark with both gold intermediate-step labels and final success labels for the same agent outputs is still missing.

hypothesis: Percent improvements reported across papers may be relative gains over different baselines or normalized aggregates, whereas average success rate is an absolute solved-fraction metric, so the same outputs can imply positive percent change without high absolute success.
mechanism: If one metric is a delta-from-baseline and the other is absolute performance, a modest absolute score can still yield a large positive percent improvement when the baseline is weak; conversely, a strong baseline can make the same absolute output look like a negative percent change.
predictions: Absolute success can stay modest while relative percent gain is positive.
Recomputing all results as absolute solved fraction reduces sign disagreements across papers.
minimal_test: On a shared eval set, generate one paired prediction table from the same model outputs and a fixed baseline's outputs. Score each item/model pair with (1) absolute success rate and (2) percent improvement relative to the baseline under the paper-specific formula. Then recompute all systems under a common absolute metric and compare whether formerly conflicting positive/negative conclusions shrink.
evidence_gap: The claim texts do not specify whether each reported percent is relative improvement, absolute percentage-point change, or an aggregate over heterogeneous datasets/baselines.

hypothesis: Some percent-based evaluations may be surface-form or behavior-pattern sensitive, whereas accuracy/success metrics may only score final answer correctness, so the same outputs diverge when one metric penalizes degenerate agent behavior and the other ignores it.
mechanism: An agent that collapses to a simple action pattern or uses suboptimal reasoning can still occasionally land on the correct final answer; a behavior-sensitive percent metric would mark this as poor, while answer-only accuracy or success may remain comparatively higher.
predictions: Behavior-collapse cases score worse on pattern-sensitive percent than on answer-only accuracy.
Filtering to items with valid reasoning traces narrows the metric gap.
minimal_test: Use a shared set of agentic-reasoning outputs with full traces from the same model. Cross-score each output with (1) answer-only accuracy or task success and (2) a behavior-sensitive metric derived from the same traces, such as percent of queries avoiding Simple Action collapse or satisfying required multi-step protocol. Compare disagreements item-by-item and after filtering out collapse cases.
evidence_gap: We lack a standardized trace-level annotation scheme showing whether the reported percent figures are tracking behavioral compliance rather than only final correctness.

hypothesis: An unreported moderator variable drives the conflicting results around multimodal on multi-step-reasoning.
mechanism: A confound in data preprocessing, prompt formatting, or decoding parameters correlates with outcome direction and is not held constant across the claims.
predictions: Holding prompt template and decoding fixed shrinks the between-claim variance by >50%.
A covariate analysis reveals prompt/decoding parameters account for the sign flip.
minimal_test: Replay all claims on CLD-1 and related benchmarks in a common harness with identical prompts and decoding settings; recompute Percent and related metrics deltas.
evidence_gap: Prompt and decoding configurations are inconsistently reported across the claims.

hypothesis: The apparent PPO/GRPO advantage flips depending on benchmark composition, with geometry/algebra-heavy sets favoring RL-trained chain-of-thought while broader multimodal suites like MathVista dilute or reverse the gain.
mechanism: RL on math reasoning can over-specialize to symbolic multi-step answer patterns; datasets with more diagram parsing, visual grounding, or heterogeneous question styles reduce the benefit relative to narrower algebra/geometry benchmarks.
predictions: Positive gap grows on GEO/ALG-style subsets
Gap shrinks on visually diverse MathVista subsets
minimal_test: Evaluate the same PPO-or-GRPO-trained model and the same base model on a unified benchmark suite stratified by problem family (algebra, geometry, diagram interpretation, mixed multimodal QA), then compare effect sizes within each stratum; if the contradiction is due to dataset composition, the sign mismatch should disappear after matching strata across papers.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO applied to RL-enhanced reasoning MLLMs

evidence_gap: The claims do not report per-category breakdowns or matched benchmark compositions, so it is unclear whether the compared papers emphasize different problem families.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by model-scale and baseline selection: PPO/GRPO shows large gains over the local base 7B model but only near-parity when compared against frontier reasoning models such as O1 or GPT-4.
mechanism: RL fine-tuning often yields substantial within-family improvements at fixed scale, but those gains may not be enough to clearly beat stronger larger-scale baselines; changing the comparator can therefore flip the narrative from strong positive to mixed.
predictions: Large gains appear versus Qwen-2.5-VL-7B
Near-zero gap appears versus O1/GPT-4-class baselines
minimal_test: Hold dataset, prompt, decoding, and evaluation fixed, then compare the same PPO-or-GRPO model against (i) its untuned base model and (ii) a stronger frontier model on the exact same examples; if baseline choice is the moderator, the contradiction should collapse into a monotonic pattern of large within-family gain but small cross-family gap.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO for post-training or RL-enhanced reasoning
scope_conditions.task: math-reasoning across text-only and multimodal settings

evidence_gap: Current claims mix different baselines and scales, but do not provide controlled head-to-head results against both base and frontier comparators on identical test sets.

hypothesis: The sign flip comes from prompt and decoding protocol differences, where PPO/GRPO benefits are amplified by reasoning-oriented generation settings but compressed under short-answer or different sampling protocols.
mechanism: RL-trained reasoning models can be highly sensitive to response format and test-time sampling; allowing longer chain-of-thought, majority voting, or higher test-time compute may reveal gains, whereas terse prompts or greedy decoding can suppress them and make results look only marginally better.
predictions: Self-consistency increases PPO/GRPO advantage
Greedy short-format decoding reduces the advantage
minimal_test: Re-run the same trained PPO-or-GRPO model and matched baseline on the same math benchmark under a factorial protocol: concise-answer vs reasoning prompt, and greedy vs multi-sample/self-consistency decoding; if protocol is the moderator, the positive and mixed claims should converge once prompt format and decoding are held constant.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO models whose policies were optimized for reasoning traces or grouped sampling

evidence_gap: The claims do not specify prompt templates, chain-of-thought exposure, sample count, or decoding strategy, leaving a major uncontrolled source of variance.

hypothesis: Reported gains for agent methods on multi-hop-QA are real but narrow, holding mainly on benchmarks whose decomposition and retrieval structure align with the agent's planning loop rather than across heterogeneous multi-hop settings.
mechanism: Agents may help when questions can be broken into a small number of explicit hops with recoverable intermediate evidence, but the advantage may shrink on noisy, implicit, or distractor-heavy multi-hop tasks.
predictions: Large gains on explicit-hop datasets
Smaller gains with distractor passages
minimal_test: Run the same agent system and a strong non-agent RAG baseline on at least 4 multi-hop-QA datasets spanning explicit bridge questions, comparison questions, temporal multi-hop, and distractor-heavy open-domain settings; report exact-match/F1, retrieval recall at each hop, calibration, and latency under fixed model, token budget, and retriever. Include per-dataset hop-count stratification and failure cases where first-hop retrieval is wrong.

scope_conditions.method: agent with iterative retrieval/planning compared to non-agent RAG under matched backbone and budget
scope_conditions.task: multi-hop-QA across bridge, comparison, temporal, and distractor-heavy benchmarks

evidence_gap: Current support is one-sided and lacks cross-benchmark robustness evidence under matched conditions, especially on adversarial and distractor-heavy multi-hop settings.

hypothesis: Some of the apparent agent advantage is an artifact of weak or mismatched baselines, with improvements driven by extra inference budget, retrieval rounds, or memory rather than agentic control itself.
mechanism: If agent systems are allowed more tool calls, longer contexts, more tokens, or specialized memory while baselines are single-shot or full-context, the observed gains can reflect compute and architecture differences instead of a robust agent effect.
predictions: Parity under equalized token budgets
Ablating planning preserves most gains
minimal_test: Construct a budget-matched ablation suite on the LOCOMO benchmark and one independent multi-hop-QA dataset: compare full agent, agent without planning, agent with fixed retrieval schedule, non-agent RAG with the same number of retrieval calls, and long-context baseline with equal wall-clock or token budget. Report accuracy/F1, p95 latency, total tokens, number of tool calls, and cost. Add a negative control where the agent's planner is replaced by random or heuristic retrieval order.

scope_conditions.method: agentic RAG or memory-based agent versus compute-matched non-agent and ablated variants
scope_conditions.task: multi-hop-QA with latency and accuracy jointly evaluated

evidence_gap: There is no clean isolation of agentic control from confounds such as extra compute, retrieval depth, memory modules, or baseline handicap.

hypothesis: Positive reports may partly reflect selective reporting because robustness failures under perturbation, retrieval corruption, and out-of-distribution hop composition have not been systematically published.
mechanism: Agent pipelines can appear strong on standard benchmarks yet be brittle to evidence ordering changes, missing intermediate facts, conflicting documents, or shifted hop structures; absent negative results would create a one-sided literature.
predictions: Performance drops with corrupted first hop
Latency rises sharply under conflicting evidence
minimal_test: Evaluate the reported agent setup under adversarial robustness probes: swap evidence order, inject top-k distractors, remove the gold first-hop passage, add contradictory passages, and shift to unseen hop counts or compositional templates. Use the original benchmark plus a held-out adversarial split. Compare against non-agent RAG and report exact-match/F1, retrieval recall by hop, error recovery rate after wrong first retrieval, and latency tails. Include null questions requiring no retrieval as a negative control for unnecessary agent actions.

scope_conditions.method: agent systems with iterative retrieval and decision-making
scope_conditions.task: multi-hop-QA under perturbation, corruption, and distribution shift

evidence_gap: Missing are adversarial evaluations, negative controls, and published failure analyses that would distinguish robust multi-hop reasoning from benchmark-specific overfitting or selective reporting.

hypothesis: The reported 'Percent' score is likely a benchmark-specific aggregate over heterogeneous subitems, while 'accuracy' is exact item-level correctness, so the same outputs can look stronger under one metric than the other.
mechanism: If MathVista's published percent is a normalized aggregate across mixed question types or weighted sections, it may compress gains from PPO-or-GRPO on some item types and dilute exact-correctness improvements that appear clearly on GEO/ALG/GPS accuracy.
predictions: On shared outputs, exact accuracy exceeds aggregate-percent deltas.
Subtask weighting changes the model ranking more under percent.
minimal_test: Collect one shared evaluation set by running Vision-R1-7B and its baseline on the same MathVista items, storing raw final answers for every item. Then cross-score those identical outputs twice: once with exact item-level accuracy, and once with the benchmark's official percent computation or section-aggregation rule. Compare whether the performance gap shrinks under percent and inspect which subcategories drive the discrepancy.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO on Vision-R1-7B-style RL-enhanced reasoning MLLMs
scope_conditions.task: multimodal math-reasoning benchmarks with mixed subtask composition

evidence_gap: The payload does not specify whether MathVista 'Percent' is simple accuracy or a weighted/aggregated benchmark score, nor whether GEO/ALG/GPS use identical answer aggregation.

hypothesis: One metric may score only the final answer string exactly, while the other may apply answer normalization or tolerant matching, so PPO-or-GRPO gains in reasoning can be hidden or amplified by surface-form sensitivity.
mechanism: RL-enhanced math models often change output style along with correctness. If one scorer canonicalizes units, punctuation, boxed answers, or equivalent numeric forms and the other does not, the same generations can receive different scores despite unchanged underlying reasoning quality.
predictions: Normalization reduces disagreement between accuracy and percent.
Format-heavy errors flip labels under one scorer but not the other.
minimal_test: Generate a paired prediction set by running Vision-R1-7B and the comparison model on a common sample drawn from GEO, ALG, GPS, and MathVista, saving exact raw outputs. Score the same outputs with two pipelines: strict exact-match accuracy and a normalized percent-style scorer that strips formatting, canonicalizes numbers/units, and accepts equivalent forms if that matches the benchmark script. Count label flips and recompute model gaps under both metrics.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO systems whose decoding produces verbose reasoning plus final answers
scope_conditions.task: math-reasoning tasks where equivalent answers can appear in multiple textual forms

evidence_gap: We lack the exact scoring scripts and whether the reported metrics differ in normalization, parser robustness, or equivalence handling.

hypothesis: The 'Percent' metric may be a coarse headline score rounded at benchmark level, whereas accuracy is reported as average over datasets, so small absolute differences can appear inconsistent because one emphasizes rank proximity and the other emphasizes magnitude of improvement.
mechanism: A 0.4-point gap on a single benchmark can coexist with a 10-point average gain across GEO/ALG/GPS if the percent metric is benchmark-level and potentially rounded, while accuracy averages per-dataset improvements that preserve larger magnitude differences. Cross-scoring the same outputs would show that the definitional gap is about aggregation granularity rather than true contradiction.
predictions: Per-dataset accuracy gaps exceed pooled benchmark-percent gaps.
Rounding or pooling changes apparent closeness more than item labels.
minimal_test: Obtain a shared prediction set by evaluating Vision-R1-7B and the baseline on one pooled set containing MathVista plus matched numbers of GEO/ALG/GPS items. Using the same raw outputs, compute item-level accuracy, dataset-wise average accuracy, and a single pooled percent score with benchmark-style rounding/reporting. Check whether model conclusions differ mainly after pooling and rounding rather than after per-item correctness scoring.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO comparisons reported across multiple math benchmarks
scope_conditions.task: math-reasoning evaluations mixing single-benchmark summaries with cross-dataset averages

evidence_gap: The payload does not reveal whether 'Percent' differs from accuracy only by reporting granularity, rounding, or benchmark-level pooling.

hypothesis: The sign of RAG's effect flips with dataset evidence structure: gains appear on standard factual-QA or single-answer conflict sets, but shrink or reverse on datasets where retrieved documents contain unresolved multi-hop ambiguity or misleading support.
mechanism: When dataset composition increases conflicting or weakly aligned evidence density, retrieval injects distractors that the generator cannot reliably reconcile; on cleaner or single-correct-answer sets, the same retrieval improves grounding and accuracy.
predictions: RAG gains are larger on CRAG and FaithEval than on RAMDocs
Conditioning on conflict density removes most cross-paper disagreement
minimal_test: Construct matched subsets from CRAG, FaithEval, AmbigDocs, and RAMDocs with the same answerability and evidence-count profile, then stratify by conflict density and number of plausible supporting passages while holding model, retriever, top-k, and prompt fixed; check whether RAG-vs-non-RAG differences align across papers once dataset composition is matched.
evidence_gap: The claims do not report a common annotation of conflict density, ambiguity, or distractor prevalence across datasets.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by prompt format for using retrieved context: structured evidence-selection or multi-agent prompts yield positive effects, while simple concatenated retrieval prompts can make added documents harmful.
mechanism: Prompting determines whether the model filters, ranks, and cites retrieved passages before answering; structured prompts convert retrieval into usable evidence, whereas raw concatenation overloads context and amplifies distractors, especially under conflicting evidence.
predictions: Structured evidence-selection prompts outperform plain concatenation on the same retrieved set
Cross-paper effect signs converge when all systems use one shared prompt template
minimal_test: Take one shared retriever output for each benchmark and run the same base model under two prompt conditions only—plain concatenation versus structured evidence-selection/reconciliation—while keeping retrieved documents, context budget, decoding, and evaluation fixed; test whether the negative result disappears under the structured prompt.
evidence_gap: The payload lacks standardized prompt templates and does not isolate whether reported gains come from retrieval itself or from orchestration/prompting around retrieval.

hypothesis: Model scale moderates RAG strongly enough to flip the sign: larger instruction-tuned models can exploit noisy retrieved evidence, while smaller models suffer from integration failures that make retrieval look less helpful or harmful.
mechanism: Large models have better evidence discrimination and conflict resolution, so retrieval improves answers; smaller models are more easily distracted by irrelevant passages and may underuse or misaggregate evidence, reducing apparent benefit.
predictions: RAG gains increase from Llama3 8B to 70B/72B under the same pipeline
Negative or weak results concentrate in smaller-scale models on noisy datasets
minimal_test: Evaluate one identical RAG pipeline across Llama3 8B, Llama3.3-70B, and Qwen2.5-72B on the same sampled factual-QA items from CRAG, FaithEval, AmbigDocs, and RAMDocs, with identical retrieval results, prompt, context length, decoding, and scorer; determine whether sign disagreement disappears after conditioning on model scale.
evidence_gap: Current claims compare different models and datasets simultaneously, so model-size effects are confounded with benchmark composition and prompting choices.

hypothesis: The contradiction arises because test-time-scaling helps on broad answer-generation evaluations but can look weak or ambiguous on refinement-specific judge evaluations where the baseline already includes strong candidate selection.
mechanism: Dataset/task composition moderates the effect sign: multilingual and physical-reasoning benchmarks reward extra search or thinking by increasing chance of one good completion, while critique-based refinement benchmarks measure incremental gain over reranking/greedy baselines that already absorb much of the test-time benefit.
predictions: Positive gains shrink on reranking-heavy refinement sets
Same method remains positive on direct-answer evaluation sets
minimal_test: Evaluate the same model and same test-time-scaling budget on two fixed benchmark families: direct-answer evaluation tasks and critique/refinement tasks. Use matched compute and identical baselines, then recompute gains relative to a common greedy baseline rather than task-specific ratios; if the contradiction disappears under fixed task composition, moderation by dataset/task composition is supported.
evidence_gap: The conflicting papers do not report a shared benchmark split that isolates direct-answer versus refinement-style items under identical compute and baseline definitions.

hypothesis: The disagreement is driven by prompt format: explicit thinking-mode or multi-sample prompts elicit latent reasoning gains, whereas critique/refinement prompts can introduce judge-dependent verbosity or overediting that masks improvement.
mechanism: Prompt structure changes the effective search space. Chain-of-thought or pass@k sampling prompts create diverse candidate trajectories that improve best-of-k accuracy, but critique-based refinement prompts may bias outputs toward stylistic changes or judge-pleasing text rather than correctness, flipping observed gains depending on format.
predictions: Thinking-mode gains drop under critique-style prompts
Pass@k gains persist under standardized prompt templates
minimal_test: Run one shared model on one shared evaluation set with three prompt templates held otherwise constant: direct answer, explicit thinking mode, and critique-refinement. Keep sample count and decoding fixed, score all outputs with the same metric, and compare whether positive versus mixed conclusions collapse once prompt format is standardized.
evidence_gap: The papers do not provide a prompt-controlled ablation that separates benefits of extra test-time compute from benefits or harms induced by different instruction templates.

hypothesis: The contradiction is due to evaluation protocol differences: pass@k and base-performance comparisons measure best-case answer discovery, while improvement-ratio metrics for judge efficacy can penalize the same underlying gains or compress them toward zero.
mechanism: Evaluation protocol moderates the sign by changing the denominator and aggregation rule. Pass@32 versus pass@1 rewards any successful sample among many, and base-versus-thinking compares raw task performance, but refinement improvement ratios normalize against reranking/greedy baselines and can make genuine absolute improvements appear small, mixed, or non-comparable.
predictions: Raw accuracy shows more positive effects than improvement ratios
Contradiction weakens under a shared metric family
minimal_test: Re-evaluate outputs from all three settings using a common protocol: report raw accuracy, mean-of-k accuracy, and calibrated cost-normalized gain on the same item set. Hold model, prompts, and sample budget fixed, then check whether papers that disagreed under pass@k versus improvement-ratio metrics agree under the harmonized protocol.
evidence_gap: There is no cross-paper re-scoring under a unified metric that maps pass@k, thinking-mode improvement, and judge-efficacy ratios onto the same outcome scale.

hypothesis: The divergence may arise because percent/accuracy are absolute outcome metrics while improvement ratio is a relative gain metric normalized by a chosen baseline.
mechanism: The same test-time-scaling outputs can look strong on absolute accuracy or percent if they solve many items, yet look weak or unstable on improvement ratio when the denominator baseline is already strong or varies across tasks/models; ratio magnifies small denominator differences and can invert comparative impressions.
predictions: High absolute scores can coincide with modest ratios.
Ratio rankings will change after swapping the baseline definition.
minimal_test: On one shared evaluation set, collect per-item outputs from the same model under test-time-scaling and its specified baseline condition (e.g., base mode or pass@1/greedy). Score the identical paired outputs twice: first with absolute percent/accuracy, then with the improvement-ratio formula using the same baseline outputs. Compare model/task rankings and inspect whether high absolute performers lose rank under ratio normalization.
evidence_gap: We lack a shared benchmark where the same outputs are reported with both absolute and ratio-based summaries under identical baselines.

hypothesis: The conflict may come from one metric rewarding any-sample success across multiple draws while another rewards the quality of the single selected final answer.
mechanism: Pass@k style accuracy increases when at least one sampled attempt is correct, so test-time-scaling with more samples looks beneficial even if answer selection or reranking is poor; percent or ratio computed on the finalized response can remain flat because they score only the chosen output, not latent successful samples.
predictions: Pass@k will rise faster than final-answer accuracy.
Items with one correct sample but wrong selected answer will drive the gap.
minimal_test: For a shared evaluation set, generate k sampled outputs per item from the same model with test-time-scaling, keep both the full sample pool and the final selected response. Cross-score these same generations with two metrics: pass@k on the pool and percent/accuracy or improvement ratio on the selected response. Measure how often pool-level success does not translate into selected-answer success.
evidence_gap: Current claims do not provide item-level paired sample pools and selected outputs needed to separate latent success from final response quality.

hypothesis: The apparent inconsistency may reflect that percent/accuracy are thresholded correctness metrics, whereas improvement ratio is a continuous aggregate sensitive to average score changes even when item correctness does not flip.
mechanism: Test-time-scaling can improve partial quality, confidence, or judge-assigned scores on many examples without crossing the correctness threshold, yielding a noticeable improvement ratio but little change in accuracy/percent; conversely a few threshold-crossing wins can boost accuracy while barely moving the average relative score.
predictions: Many outputs will gain judged quality without changing correctness.
Small numbers of threshold flips can dominate accuracy gains.
minimal_test: On a shared evaluation set, save the same model outputs before and after test-time-scaling. For each output, obtain both a binary correctness label and the continuous judge/performance score used to compute improvement ratio. Cross-score the identical outputs with both metrics, then count cases where continuous scores improve but binary correctness is unchanged versus cases with threshold flips.
evidence_gap: There is no shared item-level release linking binary correctness labels to the continuous scoring signal underlying the reported improvement ratios.

hypothesis: RAG appears beneficial in papers using domain-QA benchmarks whose answers require multi-hop reasoning over curated domain knowledge, but the effect can shrink or reverse on benchmarks dominated by direct fact lookup or perceptual grounding.
mechanism: Dataset composition moderates the sign: reasoning-heavy cases reward structured retrieval and explicit knowledge integration, whereas factual or multimodal items can saturate with strong base models and make extra retrieved context redundant or distracting.
predictions: Reasoning-tagged subsets show larger RAG gains
Lookup/perception subsets show smaller or negative gains
minimal_test: Re-evaluate a common RAG pipeline and a no/weak-RAG baseline on both CPDD and ScienceQA/CrisisMMD after stratifying examples into reasoning-intensive versus direct-lookup/perceptual bins, then compare the RAG-minus-baseline delta within each bin; if the contradiction is due to dataset composition, the cross-paper disagreement should largely disappear within matched bins.
evidence_gap: The papers do not report harmonized per-example taxonomy linking reasoning depth, modality demands, and RAG gain.

hypothesis: The reported advantage of RAG depends on retrieval recall quality, with high-recall retrieval making RAG strongly positive and lower-recall or noisier retrieval flipping the effect toward neutral or harmful.
mechanism: Retrieval recall is a sign-flipping moderator: when retrieved evidence covers the answer-bearing documents, generation is grounded and improved; when recall is poor or retrieved items are noisy, RAG injects distractors and compounds errors, especially in domain settings with sparse terminology or multimodal evidence.
predictions: Higher retrieval recall monotonically increases RAG benefit
Matched-recall systems reduce cross-paper discrepancy
minimal_test: Implement a shared retriever and corpus construction protocol across the compared setups, tune top-k to equalize oracle answer coverage on CPDD and ScienceQA/CrisisMMD, and then rerun generation with identical readers; if holding recall fixed removes most of the disagreement in RAG gains, retrieval quality is the relevant moderator.
evidence_gap: Neither paper reports directly comparable retrieval recall, oracle coverage, or noise rates under a shared corpus/retriever setup.

hypothesis: The sign of the RAG effect is moderated by inference format and decoding choices, with structured reasoning prompts and conservative decoding amplifying gains while shorter or more stochastic prompting can erase them.
mechanism: Prompt format and decoding strategy change how much the model uses retrieved evidence versus prior knowledge: explicit stepwise reasoning or agent coordination can force evidence use and improve answers, whereas terse prompts or high-temperature decoding can cause hallucinated reasoning, context neglect, or unstable aggregation that weakens RAG.
predictions: Structured evidence-grounded prompts yield larger gains
Low-temperature decoding reduces variance and disagreement
