minimal_test: Take one common backbone and retriever, then run both papers' prompt templates or closest reproductions under a fixed decoding grid (e.g., greedy vs temperature sampling) on a shared benchmark slice; if the contradiction disappears when prompt and decoding are standardized, the sign flip is attributable to inference-format differences.
evidence_gap: The claims do not expose enough detail about prompt templates, evidence formatting, agent messages, or decoding hyperparameters for direct comparison.

hypothesis: The task_type setting is the main variable flipping RAG outcomes: gains are larger for reasoning-heavy domain-QA than for factual domain-QA when the same RAG family is otherwise held fixed.
mechanism: Reasoning tasks benefit more from structured intermediate knowledge and multi-step evidence composition, whereas factual tasks saturate with direct retrieval and are more sensitive to irrelevant added context, so the same RAG augmentation can help in reasoning but show weaker or mixed effects in factual QA.
predictions: On CPDD, switching evaluation from reasoning-style questions to factual lookup with the same RAG stack will shrink the gain over baseline.
On ScienceQA or CrisisMMD, partitioning items by reasoning demand will show larger RAG deltas on multi-hop/explanatory subsets than on direct-fact subsets.
minimal_test: Take one original benchmark with heterogeneous item types, preferably ScienceQA, keep model/retriever/top_k/context length fixed, label or subset questions by task_type (factual vs reasoning), and run a parameter sweep only over task_type by evaluating the identical RAG pipeline separately on those subsets; repeat on CPDD if factual-style subsets can be constructed.
evidence_gap: The current claims compare different papers and datasets, but do not provide a within-benchmark controlled sweep isolating task_type alone.

hypothesis: The specific task_type distinction of multimodal factual QA versus text-centric diagnostic reasoning is the setting variable responsible for the divergence, not RAG per se.
mechanism: In multimodal factual QA, retrieval mainly supports grounding and alignment across modalities, while in diagnostic reasoning it supports hypothesis generation and causal chaining; these different task types change whether extra retrieved evidence reduces ambiguity or introduces distractors.
predictions: Within CrisisMMD, converting questions from direct factual prompts to explanation-required prompts with the same HM-RAG system will change the size of the RAG advantage.
Within a medical benchmark like CPDD, replacing diagnostic reasoning prompts with answer-only factual prompts will reduce the observed benefit of KG-elicited RAG.
minimal_test: On CrisisMMD or ScienceQA, keep architecture, retriever, top_k, and context budget fixed and sweep only task_type by rephrasing or selecting benchmark items into factual-answer versus explanation/reasoning versions; measure whether the RAG-minus-baseline delta changes sign or magnitude across those task_type conditions.
evidence_gap: No cited claim directly reports the same benchmark evaluated under matched multimodal or text inputs while varying only prompt-defined task_type.

hypothesis: The coarse task_type level of question difficulty or cognitive depth within domain-QA is the hidden setting that drives conflicting outcomes, with higher-depth tasks showing stronger RAG gains than lower-depth tasks.
mechanism: As cognitive depth rises, retrieved evidence and structured augmentation become more valuable because the model must connect dispersed facts; for shallow tasks, retrieval can be redundant and extra evidence can dilute answer selection, producing weaker or mixed results.
predictions: On ScienceQA, RAG improvements will be larger on harder grade 7-12 or explanation-heavy items than on easier items when all non-task settings are fixed.
On CPDD, the gain from KG-elicited RAG will increase from simpler to more complex diagnostic levels under the same model and retrieval configuration.
minimal_test: Using ScienceQA, hold the full HM-RAG setup fixed and sweep only task_type operationalized as cognitive-depth bins (e.g., simple factual, moderate inference, complex reasoning or existing grade bands if used as a task-type proxy); report RAG-vs-baseline deltas per bin. Replicate on CPDD across predefined reasoning levels if available.
evidence_gap: Existing evidence hints at depth sensitivity, but there is no controlled single-benchmark analysis showing that cognitive-depth task_type alone explains the divergence across reported outcomes.

hypothesis: L1/L2/L3 are likely graded clinical-reasoning correctness metrics that reward partially correct multi-step answers, whereas plain accuracy mostly rewards exact final-answer correctness, so RAG can look stronger on L2/L3 than on accuracy when retrieval improves reasoning structure more than endpoint selection.
mechanism: The same generated answer may contain useful intermediate evidence or partially correct diagnostic chains that earn credit under L2/L3 but still miss the final label counted by accuracy; cross-scoring the identical outputs therefore can produce gains on L2/L3 without proportional gains on accuracy.
predictions: Many accuracy=0 outputs receive nonzero L2/L3.
RAG deltas are larger on reasoning questions than on factual ones.
minimal_test: Run one RAG model and one baseline on a shared domain-QA set that mixes CPDD-style reasoning items and standard factual items; save raw answer strings for every question, then score each same prediction set with both exact accuracy and the L1/L2/L3 rubric (via original annotation guide or expert relabeling). Compare per-example score pairs and method deltas across metrics.
evidence_gap: The payload does not define L1/L2/L3 scoring rubrics or report paired per-example outputs scored by both metrics.

hypothesis: Accuracy is likely insensitive to answer justification quality, while L2/L3 measure explanation faithfulness or evidence-supported reasoning quality, so RAG methods that improve retrieved support can score better on L2/L3 even when final-answer hit rate changes little.
mechanism: Retrieval can make answers better grounded and more coherent by adding domain evidence and reducing noise, which raises rubric-based reasoning scores; however, if the model's final option selection is already saturated or still brittle, exact accuracy may not move in lockstep.
predictions: Outputs with cited evidence quality improvements show weak accuracy change.
Cross-metric correlation is lower for long justifications than short answers.
minimal_test: Using a shared held-out domain-QA set, force the same RAG model to emit final answers plus free-text rationales for every item. Preserve those identical outputs and score them twice: once with exact final-answer accuracy and once with the L1/L2/L3 rubric on the rationale/answer pair. Then bucket examples by rationale length or evidence specificity and test whether L2/L3 gains exceed accuracy gains in longer evidence-rich responses.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA with generated rationales or multi-step explanations

evidence_gap: No paired analysis shows whether L2/L3 explicitly reward faithfulness, support usage, or explanation completeness distinct from answer correctness.

hypothesis: Plain accuracy may collapse all errors into binary right/wrong, while L2 versus L3 likely separate coarse versus strict semantic correctness levels, making metric disagreement arise from answer granularity: RAG may improve broad diagnostic category ranking without consistently reaching the strictest label level.
mechanism: A retrieved context can move the model closer to the right domain concept or differential diagnosis, which earns credit on a looser level-based metric, but the same output may fail the finest-grained label needed for strict accuracy or a harder level metric. Cross-scoring identical outputs would reveal systematic near-miss patterns.
predictions: Near-miss answers cluster at high L2 but low accuracy.
Metric gaps shrink when labels are coarse-grained.
minimal_test: Take a shared evaluation set with hierarchical or fine-grained domain labels, generate one fixed prediction file from the same RAG system, and score that identical file under (a) exact label accuracy and (b) mapped coarse-to-fine L1/L2/L3 criteria derived from the original benchmark definitions or expert hierarchical relabeling. Recompute scores after collapsing labels to coarser categories to see whether the divergence between metrics narrows.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA with hierarchical, graded, or fine-grained answer spaces

evidence_gap: The provided claims do not specify whether L1/L2/L3 correspond to hierarchical label levels or semantic tolerance bands.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because the two communities frame the same reliability problem with different evaluation vocabularies: visual world knowledge emphasizes calibration and abstention behavior, while biology papers emphasize end-task accuracy/F1 after multimodal fusion and GRPO.
mechanism: Terminology drift plus metric choice can hide overlap: a conservative answering strategy or overconfidence pattern in one field is not indexed as an optimization or alignment issue in the other, so searches and citations miss each other despite shared concerns about forecasting quality.
predictions: Biology papers rarely report calibration curves or abstention rates
Adding ECE/CGA to the biology benchmark reorders some model rankings
minimal_test: Compare 20 papers per community for metric usage and terminology, then run the KEGG-derived biology benchmark with confidence reporting and compute ECE plus Correct Given Attempted for Evo2+Qwen3-4B and baselines.

scope_conditions.method: confidence calibration analysis transferred into DNA-LLM hybrid evaluation
scope_conditions.task: biological reasoning and atomic visual world knowledge evaluation

evidence_gap: There is not yet a direct side-by-side audit showing whether biology gains remain when confidence-aware metrics are added.

hypothesis: The disconnect may persist because venue separation and modality-specific priors lead each side to treat the other modality as incidental: visual world knowledge studies focus on image-text factual evaluation, while biology studies focus on DNA-text multimodal reasoning and reinforcement learning refinement.
mechanism: Methodological priors shape what counts as a contribution; multimodal alignment in biology is framed as sequence-language integration, whereas visual work treats modality alignment as perception-to-text grounding, so shared alignment failures are seen as domain-specific rather than general.
predictions: Cross-citations between multimodal biology and visual evaluation papers are near zero despite shared text-alignment language
Importing DNA-LLM style fusion into a visual benchmark improves some hard cases over text-only or image-only baselines
minimal_test: Do a citation and keyword overlap analysis across recent multimodal biology and visual world knowledge papers, then build a small fusion baseline on WorldVQA that combines visual encoder outputs with a text LLM using the hybrid fusion pattern described for DNA-LLM models.

scope_conditions.method: multimodal hybrid modeling transferred across domains
scope_conditions.task: WorldVQA-style visual world knowledge questions versus KEGG-derived reasoning

evidence_gap: Missing are controlled ablations showing whether the fusion mechanism itself, rather than domain-specific encoders, is the transferable ingredient.

hypothesis: Cross-pollination would likely be strongest if biology imported visual-world confidence diagnostics, while visual world knowledge imported GRPO-style post-training to optimize selective answering and alignment under multimodal inputs.
mechanism: Generational lag in tooling adoption may matter more than theory disagreement: visual evaluation has richer diagnostics for answer confidence, while biology currently shows stronger use of RL refinement for multimodal hybrids; each community may simply not have tested the other's mature tools on its own benchmarks.
predictions: GRPO on WorldVQA increases F-score mainly by changing attempt rate
Visual-style reliability diagrams reveal overconfidence in biology hybrids despite high F1
minimal_test: Run a small replication with GRPO fine-tuning on a WorldVQA-capable multimodal model and measure F-score, CGA, and attempt rate; separately evaluate Evo2+Qwen3-4B on biology tasks with reliability diagrams and confidence bins.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO transferred to visual evaluation; calibration tooling transferred to biology
scope_conditions.task: evaluation tasks with confidence outputs and scientific-reasoning tasks with class probabilities

evidence_gap: No published experiment yet isolates whether RL gains and confidence failures transfer across these two specific modalities under matched reporting.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by dataset composition: robustness looks improved on MATH-style perturbation benchmarks but collapses on olympiad-grade full-solution sets, flipping the apparent effect of the evaluation-method.
mechanism: Perturbed MATH items often preserve short-answer structure and familiar solution templates, so newer models can maintain answer accuracy despite wording changes; USAMO/IMO datasets require long-form proof construction and sparse partial-credit opportunities, making the same evaluation-method report much lower performance.
predictions: Within one model family, gains shrink from MATH perturbations to olympiad proofs.
Matching datasets by difficulty and answer format reduces the disagreement.
minimal_test: Evaluate the same fixed set of models with the same prompting and scoring on two controlled subsets: (i) MATH-style short-answer perturbations and (ii) olympiad-style full-solution problems matched for topic and difficulty as closely as possible; then compare whether the sign of the robustness/progress conclusion converges once dataset composition is held constant.
evidence_gap: The claims do not report a harmonized dataset split with matched difficulty, answer format, and topic coverage across papers.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by evaluation protocol differences: automated answer-match scoring can show robustness gains, while rigorous human grading of full solutions reveals large failures, reversing the conclusion.
mechanism: If one paper scores final numeric answers or permissive equivalence matches, models can appear stable under perturbation; stricter human evaluation checks validity of intermediate reasoning, proof completeness, and hidden errors, which disproportionately lowers scores on the same underlying outputs.
predictions: Regrading the same outputs with human rubrics lowers apparent robustness.
Using answer-only scoring on full-solution tasks increases agreement with the positive claim.
minimal_test: Collect model outputs on a shared problem set and score each output under two fixed protocols: exact/final-answer matching and blinded human rubric grading; if the contradiction disappears when all papers are compared under one common protocol, evaluation protocol is the moderator.
evidence_gap: The payload lacks side-by-side scores for identical outputs under both automatic and human evaluation, so protocol sensitivity is not directly observed.

hypothesis: The contradiction is driven by model scale/generation regime: newer or stronger models are robust to simple perturbations, but weaker models or stricter single-sample decoding still show substantial drops, making the sign depend on which models and decoding settings were included.
mechanism: Capability threshold effects can make perturbation robustness nonlinear: above a certain scale or with broader search, models preserve reasoning under paraphrase; below that threshold, small perturbations derail reasoning, producing the mixed and negative findings reported across papers.
predictions: Robustness improves monotonically with stronger model families or larger variants.
The contradiction narrows when all models use the same decoding budget and sample count.
minimal_test: Run a capability-controlled sweep over small and large models from the same family on the same perturbation set, fixing prompt, temperature, max tokens, and number of samples; then rerun with a common higher-search decoding budget to test whether the reported sign difference vanishes once model scale and decoding are held constant.
evidence_gap: The claims mention different model sets and failure rates but do not provide a standardized cross-scale, cross-decoding comparison on the same benchmark.

hypothesis: The apparent conflict comes from final-answer exact-match percent versus rubric-based solution accuracy measuring different parts of math reasoning.
mechanism: Percent on perturbation benchmarks can count a response as correct when the final numeric/symbolic answer matches, even if intermediate reasoning is brittle; human-evaluated accuracy on olympiad-style solutions rewards logically valid derivations and penalizes unsupported or incomplete proofs, so the same outputs can look robust by one metric and weak by the other.
predictions: Exact-match stays moderate while rubric accuracy drops on the same solutions.
Perturbation gains shrink when answers are rescored for proof validity.
minimal_test: Collect one shared set of model outputs for the same math-reasoning items, requiring full worked solutions plus final boxed answers. Cross-score each output twice: (1) exact-match percent using only the final answer string after normalization, and (2) blinded human rubric accuracy for correctness/completeness of the reasoning. Compare per-item disagreements, especially cases with correct final answers but invalid or incomplete derivations.
evidence_gap: We lack a paired evaluation where identical responses from the same models are judged by both final-answer matching and rigorous human proof scoring.

hypothesis: The mismatch is driven by aggregate percent-drop metrics over original/perturbed pairs versus absolute accuracy metrics on hard standalone problems.
mechanism: A model can show a small relative drop between original and perturbed variants because it preserves ranking across paired items, yet still have low absolute accuracy if both versions are hard. Thus percent-change metrics reward consistency under perturbation, while accuracy rewards absolute task success.
predictions: Models with low perturbation drop can still have low absolute accuracy on both versions.
Cross-metric correlation is weak when base difficulty is high.
minimal_test: Build a shared paired set of original and perturbed math problems spanning easy to olympiad-hard levels. For each model output, record correctness on the original and perturbed versions. Then compute two scores from the same outputs: (1) percent drop or robustness gap between original and perturbed correctness, and (2) absolute accuracy on the perturbed set alone. Test whether models/items with small drops nevertheless have low absolute accuracy, especially on the hardest subset.
evidence_gap: Current claims do not report robustness-gap and absolute-accuracy side by side on the same items and outputs, so difficulty could be confounding the interpretation.

hypothesis: The divergence reflects instance-weighted percent of affected problems versus error-conditioned accuracy-style summaries over only failures, causing different impressions of severity.
mechanism: One percent metric counts how many total problems fall into a perturbation-sensitive category, while another summarizes the share of total errors attributable to that category. On the same output set, a failure mode can touch many instances yet compose a small fraction of all errors if the model already makes many other mistakes; this yields mixed or conflicting conclusions.
predictions: The same failure mode looks large by total-problem percent but small by error-share.
Models with lower base accuracy show smaller error-share for the same affected-item count.
minimal_test: Using one shared set of original/modified problem outputs from the same models, label each item for the specific perturbation failure mode described in the claims. Cross-score identical outputs with two metrics: (1) percent of all problems exhibiting the failure mode, and (2) percent of all incorrect responses attributable to that failure mode. Compare model rankings and severity judgments under both denominators.
evidence_gap: We need denominator-aligned reporting from the same predictions; existing claims cite different normalizations without showing how the same labeled outputs behave under both.

hypothesis: The apparent disagreement is driven by dataset composition: multimodal gains are positive on domain-specific datasets with strong structured visual priors, but can shrink or reverse on more heterogeneous domain-QA subsets.
mechanism: Embodied scenes with reachability cues and radiology images with stable pathology patterns reward specialized multimodal inductive biases, whereas broader or less curated domain-QA mixtures dilute those cues and reduce advantage over strong generalist baselines.
predictions: Effect size is larger on tightly curated in-domain subsets
Contradiction weakens after matching label and image-type mix
minimal_test: Construct matched evaluation slices across the reported benchmarks by controlling for question type, image modality, difficulty, and answer space size; re-evaluate the same multimodal systems and baselines on these matched slices to see whether the relative ranking converges.
evidence_gap: The current claims do not report cross-paper matched subset analyses or enough dataset composition statistics to test whether image/question distributions differ enough to flip rankings.

hypothesis: The contradiction is moderated by prompt format and reasoning elicitation: multimodal models benefit when evaluation prompts request step-by-step verification, but not when prompts require short direct answers.
mechanism: Process-supervised models are optimized to externalize intermediate reasoning, so chain-of-thought or verification-style prompts amplify their strengths; direct-answer prompting can suppress those gains and favor general-purpose models tuned for concise responses.
predictions: Reasoning prompts increase multimodal advantage over GPT-4o
Direct-answer prompts reduce or invert the reported margin
minimal_test: Run all compared systems on the same held-out domain-QA set under two fixed prompt templates only: a direct-answer template and a step-by-step verification template, with all other settings held constant; check whether the paper-level disagreement disappears within each prompt condition.
evidence_gap: The payload lacks prompt templates and whether different papers allowed chain-of-thought, verification instructions, or answer formatting constraints during evaluation.

hypothesis: The disagreement is caused by evaluation protocol differences, especially metric choice and answer normalization, which can change whether multimodal specialization appears beneficial.
mechanism: Free-form reasoning metrics such as RadRScore or RaTEscore can reward partially correct clinical rationale, while exact-match or diagnosis accuracy metrics penalize verbosity and formatting variance; the same outputs can therefore rank differently under different scorers.
predictions: Rankings shift when moving from rationale-based to exact-answer scoring
Answer normalization reduces cross-paper ranking differences
minimal_test: Take a shared prediction set from the compared multimodal models and rescore it under multiple protocols used across the papers: rationale-sensitive metrics, exact diagnosis accuracy, and normalized answer extraction; test whether the contradiction in relative performance disappears when a single scoring protocol is enforced.
evidence_gap: There is no common evaluation script or report of how free-form outputs were normalized, so it is unclear whether metric/protocol differences alone account for the conflicting conclusions.

hypothesis: The responsible setting variable is task_type, with multimodal gains appearing larger on reasoning-style domain-QA than on factual diagnosis-style domain-QA.
mechanism: Reasoning tasks let multimodal systems exploit cross-modal intermediate inference and verification steps, while factual diagnosis questions are closer to label extraction where visual input alone or stronger domain priors narrow the advantage.
predictions: On the radiology benchmark, performance gaps versus GPT-4o increase from factual to reasoning settings.
Within one model family, multimodal-over-baseline gains are smallest on binary/single/multi-disease factual QA.
minimal_test: On the ChestX-Reasoner evaluation benchmark, hold model, training data, decoding, and metric fixed and sweep only task_type across factual diagnosis, mixed, and reasoning subsets; compare the multimodal model's delta over GPT-4o or its base Qwen2VL-7B within each subset.
evidence_gap: Current claims mix embodied and radiology domains and do not report a controlled within-benchmark breakdown where only task_type changes.

hypothesis: The responsible setting variable is task_type specifically along an embodied-vs-clinical factual split, where multimodal helps factual QA in embodied environments but not necessarily in factual disease-diagnosis QA.
mechanism: Embodied factual QA depends on scene grounding, spatial relations, and reachability cues that are uniquely visual, whereas clinical factual diagnosis may be dominated by learned medical priors and class frequency patterns, reducing the marginal benefit from multimodal evidence.
predictions: On an embodied QA benchmark, factual multimodal gains remain positive when task_type is factual.
On a clinical diagnosis benchmark, factual multimodal gains shrink relative to matched reasoning questions.
minimal_test: Take one original benchmark with multiple subtask labels if available, or the radiology benchmark plus an embodied benchmark, hold architecture and evaluation protocol fixed, and sweep only task_type between factual-scene-grounded and factual-diagnosis questions; measure the change in multimodal advantage over the same baseline.
evidence_gap: The factual category is overloaded across papers; we lack a harmonized taxonomy separating factual scene grounding from factual diagnosis while keeping other settings aligned.

hypothesis: The responsible setting variable is task_type granularity, where binary/single/multiple disease diagnosis subtasks attenuate multimodal advantages relative to open-ended reasoning subtasks.
mechanism: Closed-form diagnostic subtasks constrain answer space and reward calibrated classification more than multimodal explanation quality, while open-ended reasoning subtasks expose benefits from richer image-text integration and process supervision.
predictions: Within the same radiology benchmark, multimodal gains are lower on binary diagnosis than on open-ended reasoning.
As task_type shifts from classification-style to explanation-style QA, RadRScore/RaTEscore improvements increase.
minimal_test: Using the ChestX-Reasoner benchmark, keep model weights, images, prompts, and decoding fixed and sweep only task_type across binary diagnosis, single-disease diagnosis, multiple-disease diagnosis, and open-ended reasoning subsets; evaluate the multimodal system's margin over GPT-4o and Qwen2VL-7B on each subset.
evidence_gap: Reported results do not isolate whether observed differences come from answer-space granularity versus other benchmark design choices.

hypothesis: RadRScore/RaTEscore may reward clinically faithful reasoning and explanation quality, while accuracy/percent mainly reward final label exactness, so the same multimodal outputs can look stronger on one metric family than another.
mechanism: A model can produce partially correct, evidence-grounded rationales that earn high radiology-specific scores even when its final diagnosis label misses strict exact-match accuracy; conversely, terse correct labels can score well on accuracy but poorly on reasoning-sensitive metrics.
predictions: High RaTEscore outputs will include some accuracy errors.
Some accuracy-correct outputs will receive middling RadRScore.
minimal_test: Collect the exact same answer+rationale outputs from ChestX-Reasoner, Qwen2VL-7B, and GPT-4o on a shared radiology QA eval set containing binary, single-, and multiple-disease items. Score every output with all available metrics: accuracy, RadRScore, and RaTEscore. Then compare per-example rank correlations and inspect disagreement buckets where answers are accuracy-wrong but RadRScore/RaTEscore-high, versus accuracy-right but reasoning-score-low.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA, especially radiology diagnosis/reasoning with free-text rationales

evidence_gap: The claims do not specify the exact annotation rubric overlap between accuracy, RadRScore, and RaTEscore or whether the same outputs were jointly scored.

hypothesis: Overall performance / averaged improvements may be macro-aggregated across heterogeneous subtasks, whereas accuracy and percent reflect single-task or single-dataset point estimates, causing apparent inconsistency even for the same model family.
mechanism: Macro averages can upweight harder subtasks or equalize task groups, so a model that is only modestly better on one diagnosis accuracy slice can still appear strongly improved overall if it wins broadly elsewhere; alternatively, a large percent gain on one benchmark need not translate to a large overall average.
predictions: Per-subtask accuracy gains will vary much more than overall score.
Recomputing micro vs macro averages will change model ranking gaps.
minimal_test: Using one shared eval set with item-level predictions from the same multimodal models, partition examples into the reported subtask groups (for example binary, single, multiple disease, or benchmark-specific subsets). Cross-score the identical predictions with per-item accuracy and any component metrics used in the overall score, then recompute percent improvement, macro-averaged overall performance, and micro-averaged overall performance from the same outputs to see whether aggregation alone reproduces the divergence.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA with multiple subtask slices or benchmark mixtures

evidence_gap: The claims do not reveal the weighting scheme behind overall performance / averaged improvements or whether percent improvements are computed on identical sample mixes.

graph_bridge.from: overall performance, overall performance / averaged improvements, percent

hypothesis: Accuracy may be surface-form strict while RadRScore/RaTEscore or overall-performance composites may tolerate semantic equivalence and partial credit, so multimodal models with verbose domain wording can be undervalued by accuracy but rewarded by domain-aware scoring.
mechanism: In domain-QA, outputs often contain synonyms, uncertainty qualifiers, multiple findings, or explanatory text. Exact or near-exact accuracy can mark these as wrong if the canonical label format differs, while rubric-based radiology metrics can still score them as clinically acceptable or partially correct.
predictions: Verbose synonymous answers will lose more accuracy than RaTEscore.
Format-normalized answers will narrow metric disagreements.
minimal_test: On a shared radiology QA eval set, save the raw generated outputs from the same multimodal models. Score each raw output with accuracy, RadRScore, and RaTEscore. Then create a normalized version of the exact same outputs by mapping synonyms, stripping rationale text to final labels, and standardizing disease names; rescore with all metrics. If disagreement shrinks mainly after normalization for accuracy but not for RadRScore/RaTEscore, the definitional gap is surface-form sensitivity.

scope_conditions.task: domain-QA where answers are free-text or multi-span rather than fixed-choice labels

evidence_gap: The current claims do not report whether accuracy uses strict exact match, synonym normalization, or label extraction from longer multimodal responses.

hypothesis: The reported PPO-or-GRPO gains on domain-QA are real within the studied medical/clinical reasoning setups, but their apparent size is inflated by one-sided reporting from a small set of closely related domains and models.
mechanism: RL-based post-training may improve structured domain reasoning and answer calibration in expert multimodal tasks, while selective publication of only strongest-domain results exaggerates average gains when weaker or null domains are omitted.
predictions: Held-out clinical domains show smaller but still positive gains over SFT.
Complete per-domain tables reveal at least one near-zero or negative domain.
minimal_test: Run a preregistered multi-domain benchmark using the same base models and training budget, comparing SFT vs PPO/GRPO/DRPO across all available clinical and non-clinical domain-QA subsets, and release per-dataset, per-seed results including domains with null effects. Exact missing data: full domain-by-domain metrics for every trained checkpoint, at least 5 random seeds per method, confidence intervals, and an evaluation set that includes unseen domains rather than only MRI/dermoscopy/vision subsets.

scope_conditions.method: PPO, GRPO, and DRPO compared under matched compute/data budgets
scope_conditions.task: domain-QA spanning medical vision-language reasoning, clinical multimodal reasoning, and unseen held-out domains

evidence_gap: Current claims are uniformly positive and concentrated in a few medical/clinical settings, with no comprehensive negative results, no seed variance, and no all-domain reporting.

hypothesis: Some of the apparent advantage of PPO-or-GRPO on domain-QA is an artifact of confounding from model scale, data volume, or domain-specialized curation rather than the RL objective itself.
mechanism: If RL-trained systems also differ in parameter count, instruction corpus size, or domain filtering, then gains attributed to PPO/GRPO may actually come from larger models or better data; cross-paper comparisons can further hide this confound.
predictions: Under fixed model size and identical training data, RL gains shrink substantially.
A scale-matched SFT baseline recovers much of the reported F1/accuracy improvement.
minimal_test: Conduct a factorial ablation with identical base architecture, identical instruction data, identical number of updates, and matched compute: SFT-only, PPO/GRPO, and DRPO, each at the same parameter size (for example 7B vs 7B and 32B vs 32B) and with/without domain-aware data curation. Exact missing data: same-model same-data comparisons, training-token counts, reward definitions, compute budgets, and per-condition test metrics on the same domain-QA benchmark; additionally include a negative control where DRPO is replaced by shuffled or domain-agnostic reward grouping.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO variants versus SFT under strict compute/data/scale matching
scope_conditions.task: domain-QA where current evidence mixes medical VLM QA, multimodal clinical reasoning, and classification-like diagnostic summaries

evidence_gap: Existing positive claims include cross-model and cross-method comparisons that do not isolate the causal contribution of the RL objective from scale, curation, or data quantity.

hypothesis: The reported PPO-or-GRPO benefits may fail under adversarial robustness and distribution-shift settings, meaning the effect is not yet established as robust domain-QA improvement even if in-distribution metrics rise.
mechanism: RL optimization can overfit to narrow reward proxies, benchmark style, or annotation regularities, producing better average Accuracy/F1 on familiar test sets while degrading under wording shifts, rare modalities, abstention decisions, or out-of-distribution cases.
predictions: Performance drops more sharply than SFT under paraphrase and OOD modality shift.
RL models show higher error rates on adversarially irrelevant visual/text cues.
minimal_test: Evaluate the same PPO/GRPO/DRPO and SFT checkpoints on a robustness suite built from the original domain-QA tasks: paraphrased prompts, label-preserving counterfactuals, irrelevant-context insertion, modality corruption, rare-disease or rare-device slices, and explicit abstention/calibration tests. Exact missing data: per-slice Accuracy/F1, calibration error, abstention AUROC, and failure cases for both in-distribution and adversarial/OOD splits; include negative controls where answerable questions are replaced with unanswerable or contradiction-injected items to test reward hacking.

scope_conditions.method: PPO-or-GRPO checkpoints evaluated without further tuning against SFT baselines
scope_conditions.task: domain-QA robustness for medical and clinical multimodal reasoning, especially OOD and adversarial variants

evidence_gap: No negative/robustness evidence is provided for adversarial prompts, distribution shift, calibration, or unanswerable cases, so current support is limited to favorable in-distribution reports.

hypothesis: Agentic gains on code-generation are real but concentrated in repeated-sampling and self-repair regimes, so reported wins mostly reflect extra search budget rather than a generally stronger problem-solving method.
mechanism: Agents generate many candidate programs and use execution feedback to prune failures, which improves pass metrics when budget is high but may vanish under matched token, wall-clock, and sample-count constraints.
predictions: Advantage shrinks under matched inference budget.
Single-shot accuracy shows much smaller gains.
minimal_test: Run AgentCoder and SEIDR against strong non-agent baselines on HumanEval, MBPP, HumanEval-C++, and PSB2 with exact budget matching: same base model, same context window, same max generated tokens, same wall-clock cap, and same number of program executions. Report pass@1, pass@k for k={5,20,100}, solved-at-least-once, variance over at least 10 seeds, and cost-normalized accuracy. Include a negative control where the baseline receives the same number of independent samples without agent coordination.

scope_conditions.method: agent vs non-agent with identical base model and inference budget
scope_conditions.task: code-generation on HumanEval, MBPP, HumanEval-C++, and PSB2

evidence_gap: Current claims emphasize high pass@100 and solved-at-least-once outcomes but lack budget-matched comparisons and low-k metrics that would separate true reasoning gains from search effects.

hypothesis: The positive pattern is partly an artifact of benchmark saturation and contamination-sensitive evaluation, with agents benefiting disproportionately on canonical public coding sets but not necessarily on fresh or adversarial tasks.
mechanism: Popular benchmarks like HumanEval and MBPP may contain highly stereotyped tasks or training-set overlap; agent loops can exploit familiar solution templates and unit-test feedback, inflating apparent robustness.
predictions: Performance drops more on fresh hidden tests.
Near-duplicate removal reduces agent lead.
minimal_test: Evaluate the same agent systems and matched non-agent baselines on contamination-controlled splits: deduplicated HumanEval/MBPP, newly authored hidden problems, temporally newer coding tasks, and adversarial rewrites with changed identifiers/spec wording but identical semantics. For each problem, keep secret held-out tests separate from any visible execution feedback, and compare pass@1/pass@k plus failure modes before and after near-duplicate filtering.

scope_conditions.method: agent systems using execution feedback or iterative refinement
scope_conditions.task: code-generation under contamination-controlled and adversarially rewritten benchmarks

evidence_gap: Missing are out-of-distribution and contamination-resistant evaluations, along with hidden-test protocols that would show whether the observed gains transfer beyond well-known public benchmarks.

hypothesis: Agentic improvements are real on average but brittle to tool-chain and environment assumptions that have not been stress-tested, so the current evidence may overstate robustness across languages, runtimes, and feedback quality.
mechanism: Agents rely on execution results, compiler messages, and iterative repair; if those signals are noisy, delayed, sandboxed, or misleading, the coordination loop may fail or even underperform direct generation.
predictions: Noisy execution feedback causes larger degradation.
Cross-language transfer is uneven under same protocol.
minimal_test: Probe AgentCoder and SEIDR with adversarial environment ablations: remove compiler stderr details, inject flaky tests or misleading partial feedback, limit execution rounds, vary sandbox restrictions, and compare Python vs C++ under identical repair budgets. Add negative controls where feedback is randomly permuted across attempts or replaced with dummy success/failure signals to test whether the agent is genuinely using informative feedback. Measure pass@1, pass@k, solved-at-least-once, and error-category shifts.

scope_conditions.method: agent coding systems with tool use, execution, and self-repair loops
scope_conditions.task: code-generation across Python and C++ with varied runtime feedback conditions

evidence_gap: The literature cited here lacks robustness checks on feedback quality, runtime constraints, and adversarial tool settings, so it is unclear whether the reported gains survive realistic deployment perturbations.
